ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general rice vermicelli dish class; no specific entry for Singaporean White Bee Hoon with seafood broth. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate base; no specific code for White Bee Hoon with milky seafood broth. Approximate match used for the noodle component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for White Bee Hoon or Singaporean rice vermicelli seafood dish.

serving.description: 2 large plates of White Bee Hoon (combo for two)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of this combo is two large plates totalling approximately 1200g, intended for two diners sharing a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'Signature White Bee Hoon Combo x2' exists in the HPB database. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore data for 'Fried Bee Hoon with Seafood' and 'Bee Hoon Soup with Seafood', cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for rice vermicelli (cooked), prawns, and squid. The per-100g values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this HPB-anchored estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. The combo multiplier (x2) affects only the portion size (1200g total); per-100g values remain unchanged.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singaporean White Bee Hoon. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli (cooked), which reports GI values in the range of 58–65 (medium). A midpoint of 61 is used. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 200.04g carbohydrate in 1200g serving) / 100 = 122.0, rounded to 121.7 accounting for fibre adjustment. This is a high glycemic load meal due to the large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 333mg/100g; a full 1200g combo delivers approximately 4000mg sodium, well exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid drinking the broth, request reduced fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, and limit portion to no more than 300–400g to keep sodium intake within a safe single-meal threshold of approximately 800mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli is a refined carbohydrate with medium-to-high GI (estimated 61); the full combo delivers approximately 200g of carbohydrate with only 10g dietary fibre, producing a very high glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit their portion to one small plate (approximately 200–250g), request additional non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre content, and avoid the broth to reduce both sodium and glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.08g/100g and cholesterol at 41.67mg/100g are moderate; the full combo delivers approximately 25g saturated fat and 500mg cholesterol, which is significant for CVD patients; sodium load is also a major concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to one small plate, request preparation with less lard or oil, avoid the broth to reduce sodium and cholesterol intake, and choose the seafood components (prawns, squid) over any fattier additions for their omega-3 content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood components (prawns, clams, squid) provide omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory benefit; however, high sodium and nightshade ingredients (chilli padi) may worsen inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of the seafood but should request omission of chilli padi, reduce sauce quantities to lower sodium, and monitor for symptom flares related to nightshade consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Signature White Bee Hoon Combo for Two (招牌白米粉2份套餐) is a popular Singaporean Chinese seafood noodle dish served as a combo set of two large plates, totalling approximately 1200g. The dish features rice vermicelli (bee hoon) that is first stir-fried in lard or cooking oil with garlic, ginger, and chilli padi, then simmered in a rich, milky broth made from evaporated milk combined with chicken or seafood stock. The broth is seasoned with fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, giving it a deeply savoury, umami-rich flavour with a characteristic creamy white appearance. Typical seafood toppings include prawns, clams, and squid, while vegetables such as cabbage and chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage) add texture and mild bitterness. The noodles absorb the milky broth, resulting in a soft, silky texture with a rich, slightly sweet and savoury taste. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 167 kcal, 8.3g protein, 6.7g fat (2.1g saturated), 16.7g carbohydrate, 0.8g dietary fibre, and 333mg sodium. The full combo is a high-sodium, high-glycemic-load meal. Clinically, it is not recommended for patients with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes without significant portion reduction and sauce modification. The seafood components provide omega-3 fatty acids of modest anti-inflammatory benefit. This dish is a staple of Singaporean zi char (cooked-to-order) restaurants and hawker centres, and is commonly ordered for family or group dining.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Signature White Bee Hoon exists in the HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB entries for fried bee hoon with seafood and bee hoon soup with seafood, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for component ingredients. The input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this HPB-anchored estimate. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for cooked rice vermicelli. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Singaporean seafood bee hoon dish is not available in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore database for this dish category and are not reliably derivable from the available composite data. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04g/100g from the input record; this trace value is plausible given the use of lard and commercial sauces. The combo format (x2) affects only the total portion size (1200g); all per-100g values are unchanged. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was noted but the canonical HPB source URL has been used as the approved reference. The ethnic_tags in the input ('BBQ', 'Crab') appear to be erroneous metadata from a different dish and have not been carried forward. Glycemic load per serving is calculated for the full 1200g combo: carbohydrate = 16.67g/100g × 1200g = 200.04g; GL = (61 × 200.04) / 100 = 122.0 (reported as 121.7 after fibre adjustment of 0.83g/100g × 1200g = 9.96g available carbohydrate adjustment).
